tldr: Demonstrates how to create and run LLM benchmark tasks using the Kaggle Benchmarks (kbench) library, featuring hard-rule assertions, LLM-as-a-judge evaluation, and structured output testing across English and Bengali prompts.
summary: This notebook serves as a practical tutorial for the Kaggle Benchmarks platform, showing how to define custom evaluation tasks for frontier LLMs. It demonstrates three distinct task types: keyword-based hard assertions, LLM-as-a-judge quality assessment, and boolean schema validation for legal reasoning. The author validates task execution using kbench.llm placeholders and explicitly tested models like google/gemini-2.5-flash. Readers learn how to structure prompts, implement automated grading via kbench.assertions, and leverage the platform's UI for model swapping and leaderboard submission.
extensive_summary:
## Introduction to Kaggle Benchmarks
The notebook introduces the `kaggle_benchmarks` (kbench) library, designed for creating custom cognitive ability benchmarks on the Kaggle Benchmarks platform. It emphasizes that tasks must be defined via the platform's UI to avoid environment variable errors, then provides code snippets for local/remote execution.

## Task Definition & Hard Assertions
The first example defines a task named "What is mpwolke Kaggler?" using the `@kbench.task` decorator. It prompts an LLM, then applies a fast, cheap hard rule via `kbench.assertions.assert_in("platform", response.lower())`. It also demonstrates an advanced LLM-as-a-judge evaluation using `kbench.assertions.assess_response_with_judge`, passing specific criteria and iterating through `assessment.results` to assert success.

## Structured Output & Legal Reasoning Test
A second task, "Better Call Saul", tests LLM comprehension of modified legal text. It fetches the Apache 2.0 license, uses `re.sub` to inject a clause about "indefinite and unconditional service", and prompts the LLM with `schema=bool` to return a boolean judgment. The notebook uses `chats.new` to separate contexts and `assertions.assert_true`/`assert_false` to validate the model's reasoning against the original vs. modified license.

## Multilingual Evaluation
A third task tests Bengali comprehension (`Kaggle কী?`). It follows the same pattern: prompt, hard rule assertion for the keyword "platform", and LLM-judge criteria in Bengali. The author notes that assertions passed successfully in Bengali, though earlier tests with `google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite` failed to recognize the author's identity.

## Execution & Platform Integration
Tasks are executed via `.run(kbench.llm)`, which acts as a placeholder for automatic model swapping in the Kaggle UI. The notebook concludes by directing users to save tasks to the leaderboard and use `%autopilot` for task generation, noting that no external datasets were required for this initial exploration.
what_worked: LLM-as-a-judge assertions and hard keyword checks passed successfully in Bengali
Using kbench.llm as a placeholder enables seamless model swapping via the Kaggle UI
what_did_not_work: google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite failed to recognize the author's identity when asked "What is mpwolke Kaggler?"
The author notes they did not fully understand the "Better call Saul" reference
notable_techniques: Using kbench.llm as a UI-compatible placeholder for dynamic model swapping
Applying schema=bool to force structured boolean outputs from LLMs
Combining hard-rule keyword assertions with LLM-as-a-judge criteria for multi-tier evaluation
Modifying open-source license text via regex to test LLM legal reasoning boundaries
